extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property requires that flipping confusion from false to true never lowers acuity. It is violated.

confusion appears in only one rule: [interaction_drop, priority 100] IF (leg_swelling AND dyspnea AND severe_headache AND chest_pain AND sweating AND vision_loss AND stiff_neck AND syncope AND fever AND pallor AND dizziness AND confusion) THEN acuity := self_care. No rule raises acuity based on confusion; the only confusion-dependent rule lowers it, and it has the dominating priority (100, far above the priority-3 emergent rules).

Counterexample (the witness, with confusion=true): all of leg_swelling, dyspnea, severe_headache, chest_pain, sweating, vision_loss, stiff_neck, syncope, fever, pallor, dizziness = true; palpitations = false; confusion = true.

Compare the two inputs differing only in confusion:
- confusion=false: interaction_drop does not fire (its conjunction is false). The highest-priority firing rules are the priority-3 emergent rules (pallor, dyspnea, vision_loss, chest_pain, syncope), so acuity = emergent.
- confusion=true (witness): now all 12 conjuncts of interaction_drop hold, so it fires with priority 100, which beats every priority-3 rule. acuity = self_care.

Enum order self_care(0) < routine(1) < urgent(2) < emergent(3): acuity drops from emergent to self_care when confusion is set true. This lowers acuity, violating monotonicity. The reported witness_assignment is the violating (confusion=true) input.
